name: Is installing a new door a realistic DIY project, or should I hire a pro?
headline: Is installing a new door a realistic DIY project, or should I hire a pro?

mainEntity.name: Is installing a new door a realistic DIY project, or should I hire a pro?
mainEntity.text: Is installing a new door a realistic DIY project, or should I hire a pro?

acceptedAnswer.text: If you’re handy and patient, you can install a prehung door yourself, but it’s not as easy as online videos make it look. The biggest challenge is getting the door frame perfectly level, plumb, and square so the door closes smoothly and seals well. Small mistakes can lead to drafts, leaks, and a door that sticks. DIY works best when you’re replacing an interior door with the same size prehung unit and the existing framing is in good shape. For exterior doors, especially in stucco or older coastal homes, you may run into hidden issues like rot, uneven framing, or water damage. That’s when pro experience really helps. If you try it yourself, read through the full instructions first, have shims, screws, level, and foam insulation ready, and expect to spend most of a day on your first door. Take your time adjusting before you seal everything up. Don’t rush to install trim until you’re sure the door swings and latches correctly. A common pitfall is thinking you can “fix” a crooked door with just the hinges. In reality, you usually need to adjust the entire frame. Another risk is cutting into stucco or siding without understanding how to flash and waterproof the opening. If you’re unsure, or it’s a main entry door that must be secure and weather-tight, it’s often safer and cheaper in the long run to hire a professional installer.
